h1: The AERB Licensing Process for Advanced Reactors and SMRs in India after the SHANTI Act 2025
meta_title: AERB Licensing for SMRs in India: Post-SHANTI Guide
meta_description: How the AERB licenses advanced reactors and SMRs in India after the SHANTI Act 2025. Consent stages, safety codes, SAR expectations, inspection, PSR, decommissioning.
tldr: The AERB licensing process for a nuclear power plant in India is a stage-wise consent chain covering siting, construction, commissioning, operation and decommissioning, each issued only after a multi-tier review against AERB Safety Codes. Following the SHANTI Act 2025, which received Presidential assent on 20 December 2025, the AERB has become a statutory regulator with full powers to inspect, direct, suspend and license private operators, foreign vendors and SMR developers on the same graded technical basis applied to NPCIL.
canonical_summary:
Until December 2025 the AERB drew its authority from a 1983 executive order under the Atomic Energy Act 1962. The Sustainable Harnessing and Advancement of Nuclear Energy for Transforming India Act 2025 (SHANTI Act 2025), passed by the Rajya Sabha on 18 December 2025 and notified after Presidential assent on 20 December 2025, repealed the Atomic Energy Act 1962 and the Civil Liability for Nuclear Damage Act 2010, gave the AERB statutory standing, and opened nuclear construction, ownership and operation to private and foreign entities subject to AERB licensing.

The consent architecture itself is largely unchanged in shape: the AERB still issues separate authorisations for siting, construction (with three sub-clearance stages), commissioning (including first approach to criticality), operation and decommissioning, anchored by the safety code AERB/SC/G on Regulation of Nuclear and Radiation Facilities, the siting code AERB/NF/SC/S, the design codes AERB/NPP-PHWR/SC/D and AERB/NPP-LWR/SC/D, and the operation code AERB/NPP/SC/O. What changes for advanced reactors and Bharat Small Modular Reactors is the population of applicants: NPCIL is no longer the sole licensee, and Holtec, Westinghouse, GE Hitachi, NuScale and their Indian JV partners must now satisfy these codes directly.

The AERB has confirmed publicly that its existing framework is broadly applicable to SMRs through a graded approach, with technology-specific provisions being developed for modular construction, passive safety systems and below-grade integral pressurised water designs. The first Bharat SMR-200 unit is proposed at Tarapur (Maharashtra) with siting submissions to AERB expected during 2026, while large reactor projects at Kudankulam Units 5 and 6, Kakrapar Units 3 and 4, Mahi Banswara, Gorakhpur Haryana, Kaiga and Kalpakkam continue to move through the standard stage-wise consent chain.

sections.heading: AERB as a Statutory Regulator under the SHANTI Act 2025
paragraphs: The Atomic Energy Regulatory Board was constituted on 15 November 1983 by a Government of India executive order under Section 27 of the Atomic Energy Act 1962. That arrangement was repeatedly flagged by IAEA Integrated Regulatory Review Service (IRRS) missions, including the 2015 mission, as a gap in formal independence from the operator and from the Department of Atomic Energy.

The SHANTI Act 2025 settles that question. Chapter III of the Act establishes the AERB as a statutory body with its own legal personality, with a Chairperson and a whole-time member who must be persons of eminence in nuclear energy and up to seven part-time members appointed by the Central Government on the recommendation of a search-cum-selection committee.

Powers explicitly granted to the AERB under the Act include framing safety rules and codes, issuing all classes of regulatory consent (licence, registration, authorisation), conducting regulatory inspections, investigating incidents, issuing binding directions, imposing financial penalties, and suspending or revoking consents for non-compliance. The AERB also takes over the role of competent authority under the Radiation Protection Rules 2004 (now read into the SHANTI framework).
bullets: Statutory mandate covers all nuclear facilities, radiation facilities and radioactive sources in India.
AERB reports to Parliament through an Annual Report and is functionally separated from the Department of Atomic Energy.
All earlier consents issued under the Atomic Energy Act 1962 are deemed to continue under the SHANTI Act.

sections.heading: The Five-Stage Consent Chain for a Nuclear Power Plant
paragraphs: AERB applies a stage-wise consenting process described in AERB/NPP&RR/SG/G-1, Consenting Process for Nuclear Power Plants and Research Reactors. Each consent is independent and conditional, and the applicant must demonstrate fresh compliance to move to the next stage.

Siting Consent is the first authorisation. The applicant submits a Site Evaluation Report covering geotechnical, hydrogeological, seismic, meteorological, radiological background, demographic and emergency planning studies. Mahi Banswara received its siting consent in May 2025 and Gorakhpur Haryana received it in July 2015.

Construction Consent follows the safety review of the design. It is granted in three internal sub-stages: clearance for start of excavation, clearance for first pour of concrete for the main plant buildings, and clearance for erection of major equipment such as the reactor pressure vessel and steam generators. Kudankulam Units 5 and 6 received the Major Equipment Erection clearance on 1 May 2026 after multi-tier design safety review by AERB.

Commissioning Authorisation is granted in phases (Phase A pre-operational tests, Phase B hot conditioning, Phase C fuel loading and approach to criticality, Phase D low-power physics tests, Phase E power ascension). AERB issued the First Approach to Criticality clearance for Kakrapar Unit 4 by order dated 11 December 2023; first criticality was achieved on 17 December 2023.

Operating Licence is issued for an initial period of five years and is renewable subject to safety performance review. AERB granted the formal Licence for Operation of KAPS Units 3 and 4 on 3 July 2025.
Decommissioning Authorisation is the final consent, governed by AERB Safety Manual AERB/SM/DECOM-1 and the radioactive waste management code AERB/NRF/SC/RW.

sections.heading: AERB Safety Codes that Govern Each Stage
paragraphs: The umbrella code is AERB/SC/G, Regulation of Nuclear and Radiation Facilities, which sets out the minimum obligations a facility must meet to qualify for each regulatory consent. Below it sit the stage-specific codes and the topical safety guides.

Siting is governed by AERB/NF/SC/S (Rev. 1), Site Evaluation of Nuclear Facilities, which mirrors IAEA NS-R-3 (2003). Design is split by technology: AERB/NPP-PHWR/SC/D (Rev. 1) applies to indigenous 220 and 700 MWe pressurised heavy water reactors at Tarapur, Kaiga, Rajasthan, Kakrapar, Mahi Banswara and Gorakhpur Haryana. AERB/NPP-LWR/SC/D applies to light water reactors at Kudankulam and any future Westinghouse AP1000, Holtec SMR-300 or NuScale installations.

Operation is governed by AERB/NPP/SC/O (Rev. 1), which supersedes the 1989 SC/O and covers start-up, power operation, shutdown, maintenance, testing, refuelling, operational safety experience feedback, plant life management and probabilistic safety assessment. Radioactive waste from operating plants is regulated under AERB/NRF/SC/RW, Management of Radioactive Waste.

Regulatory inspection and enforcement is conducted under safety guide AERB/SG/G-4 and the supporting manual AERB/NPP&RR/SM/G-1, both of which apply equally to NPCIL-operated stations and to future privately operated SMRs.
bullets: AERB/SC/G ,  Regulation of Nuclear and Radiation Facilities (umbrella).
AERB/NF/SC/S ,  Site Evaluation of Nuclear Facilities.
AERB/NPP-PHWR/SC/D ,  Design of Pressurised Heavy Water Reactors.
AERB/NPP-LWR/SC/D ,  Design of Light Water Reactors.
AERB/NPP/SC/O ,  Operation of Nuclear Power Plants.

sections.heading: Safety Analysis Report Expectations at Each Stage
paragraphs: The Safety Analysis Report (SAR) is the central technical submission for every consent. AERB expects a progressive build-up: a Site Safety Report at the siting stage, a Preliminary Safety Analysis Report (PSAR) at the construction stage, a Pre-Operational Safety Analysis Report (PoSAR) before fuel loading, and a Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) before the operating licence is granted.

The PSAR must address the full design basis, the postulated initiating events listed in the PHWR or LWR design safety guides (AERB/NPP-PHWR/SG/D-1 through D-19 series), the deterministic safety analysis envelope, the Level 1 probabilistic safety assessment for internal events, and the design extension conditions added after Fukushima.

The FSAR is the as-built version of the PSAR. It must demonstrate plant response to design basis accidents and design extension conditions and confirm that radiation doses to workers and the public remain within the dose limits specified in the AERB Safety Code on Radiation Protection. The FSAR review is the final technical gate before the operating licence is issued.
bullets: Preliminary SAR: at construction consent and before first pour of concrete.
Pre-Operational SAR: before fuel loading clearance.
Final SAR: before the operating licence and renewed at every PSR cycle.

sections.heading: SMR-Specific Provisions Emerging in 2026
paragraphs: AERB has publicly confirmed, in response to questions in Parliament and in its 2025 and 2026 communications, that its existing regulatory framework can be applied to advanced reactors and small modular reactors on a graded basis. The principal additions being developed for the Bharat Small Modular Reactor (BSMR-200) and for foreign SMR designs concern modular factory fabrication, transport of complete reactor modules, multi-module siting, passive safety system reliability, and below-grade integral pressurised water configurations.

The BSMR-200 is a 220 MWe derivative of the IPHWR-220, jointly developed by BARC and NPCIL. The Department of Atomic Energy has confirmed that the lead BSMR-200 unit is proposed for the Tarapur site in Maharashtra. NPCIL is preparing to invite bids in the 2026 financial year. The siting submission to AERB is the first regulatory milestone.

For higher-power advanced reactors such as the indigenous BSR-200 light water SMR variant, the Holtec SMR-300, and any future Westinghouse AP300 or NuScale VOYGR proposals, AERB will apply the LWR design code AERB/NPP-LWR/SC/D with technology-specific addenda. The Maharashtra and Andhra Pradesh state governments have already proposed candidate sites.

sections.heading: Inspection Regime: Vendor Premises and On-Site
paragraphs: AERB conducts regulatory inspections under safety guide AERB/SG/G-4 and manual AERB/NPP&RR/SM/G-1. Inspection categories include routine planned inspections, reactive inspections triggered by reportable events, team inspections at major commissioning milestones, and resident inspector activity at operating stations such as Tarapur, Kaiga, Rajasthan, Kakrapar and Kudankulam.

For advanced reactors and SMRs the inspection footprint extends materially upstream into vendor premises. Construction Quality Assurance under AERB/NPP/SG/QA-1 requires AERB witness of major fabrication holds at the vendor factory. For a modular SMR this includes pressure vessel forging, integral steam generator fabrication, reactor module assembly, hydrostatic and helium leak testing, and module transport readiness. Vendors fabricating outside India will need a documented inspection access protocol with AERB before construction consent is issued.

Enforcement actions available under the SHANTI Act 2025 include written directions, additional licence conditions, monetary penalties, suspension of consent, and revocation of consent. Operating events must be reported to AERB within timelines set by the Reportable Event reporting requirements that accompany the operating licence.

sections.heading: Periodic Safety Review and Licence Renewal
paragraphs: The operating licence is granted for a period of five years. Renewal is conditional on a Periodic Safety Review (PSR) carried out at ten-year intervals under the AERB safety guide on Periodic Safety Review for Nuclear Power Plants.

The PSR follows the fourteen safety factors framework derived from IAEA SSG-25. It reviews plant design against current standards, actual condition of structures, systems and components, equipment qualification, ageing management, deterministic and probabilistic safety analyses, hazard re-evaluation, safety performance, use of operating experience, organisation and management, procedures, the human factor, emergency planning, and radiological impact on the environment.

Renewal of the operating licence is granted by AERB only after closure of all open items from the PSR and after acceptance of the updated FSAR. PSR is also the regulatory vehicle through which long-term operation beyond the original design life of 40 years is approved, as is being done for the older Tarapur and Rajasthan units.

sections.heading: Decommissioning and Post-Operational Waste Management
paragraphs: Decommissioning consent is the final authorisation in the chain. The applicant must submit a Decommissioning Plan covering strategy (immediate dismantling, deferred dismantling, or entombment), spent fuel removal, decontamination, radioactive waste characterisation, dose budgets, and end-state criteria.

Technical requirements are set out in AERB Safety Manual AERB/SM/DECOM-1, in the safety guide AERB/NPP&RR/SG/RW-8 on Decommissioning of Nuclear Facilities, and in the radioactive waste code AERB/NRF/SC/RW. Predisposal management of low and intermediate level waste follows AERB/NRF/SG/RW-2, and near surface disposal follows AERB/NRF/SG/RW-4.

Operators must also maintain a Decommissioning Financial Provision under the SHANTI Act 2025 from the moment the operating licence is granted, accumulated through tariff or sinking fund arrangements. For private and foreign operators this provision is a licence condition: failure to maintain it is a ground for suspension of the operating consent.

sections.heading: What Private Operators and Foreign Vendors Should Do Differently
paragraphs: Foreign reactor vendors and Indian private operators new to the AERB system should plan for a longer pre-application engagement than is typical under the US Nuclear Regulatory Commission or the Canadian Nuclear Safety Commission. AERB does not yet offer a formal pre-application Vendor Design Review akin to CNSC, although the SHANTI Act 2025 contemplates a similar mechanism in the rules expected during 2026 and 2027.

Practical recommendations: engage AERB during the conceptual siting study rather than at submission of the Site Safety Report; build the PSAR around AERB design codes from the outset rather than adapting an NRC SAR (the Indian PSAR conventions for postulated initiating events, single failure criterion and ECCS performance criteria are stricter in places than NRC Part 50 Appendix A); secure an inspection access protocol for any non-Indian fabrication site before construction; budget for AERB witness of vendor factory holds; and align the decommissioning financial provision with the licence conditions from day one.

Compliance teams should map their design and operating procedures to the AERB code hierarchy. Invariant works with Indian SMR developers, AERB consultants and foreign vendors to maintain that mapping continuously, including PSAR clause-to-evidence traceability, vendor inspection scheduling and reportable event triage.

faqs.question: What is the AERB and what changed under the SHANTI Act 2025?
faqs.answer: The Atomic Energy Regulatory Board is the Indian nuclear safety regulator, constituted in November 1983 by executive order under the Atomic Energy Act 1962. The SHANTI Act 2025, which received Presidential assent on 20 December 2025, repealed the Atomic Energy Act 1962 and the Civil Liability for Nuclear Damage Act 2010, and re-established the AERB as a statutory body. The AERB now has explicit statutory powers to grant, modify, suspend and revoke consents, conduct inspections, investigate incidents, impose penalties, and regulate private and foreign operators. The Board comprises a Chairperson, one whole-time member and up to seven part-time members appointed by the Central Government on the recommendation of a search-cum-selection committee.

faqs.question: What are the stages of AERB consent for a nuclear power plant?
faqs.answer: AERB issues consent in five stages under the consenting process guide AERB/NPP&RR/SG/G-1. The stages are: siting consent, after review of the Site Evaluation Report; construction consent, granted in three sub-clearances for start of excavation, first pour of concrete and erection of major equipment; commissioning authorisation, granted phase by phase culminating in the clearance for first approach to criticality and low-power physics tests; operating licence, valid for five years and renewable; and decommissioning consent at end of life. Each consent is independent and requires fresh demonstration of compliance with the relevant AERB Safety Code.

faqs.question: Which AERB Safety Code governs the design of a PHWR in India?
faqs.answer: Design of pressurised heavy water reactors is governed by AERB Safety Code AERB/NPP-PHWR/SC/D (Rev. 1), supported by the AERB/NPP-PHWR/SG/D series of safety guides (D-1 on safety classification and seismic categorisation, D-8 on primary heat transport system, D-10 on safety systems, D-19 on safety analysis and others). These codes apply to the indigenous 220 MWe and 700 MWe units at Tarapur, Kaiga, Rajasthan, Kakrapar, Mahi Banswara and Gorakhpur Haryana, and to the lead Bharat SMR-200 derivative of the IPHWR-220 planned for Tarapur.

faqs.question: Which AERB Safety Code governs light water reactors and SMRs?
faqs.answer: Design of light water reactors is governed by AERB Safety Code AERB/NPP-LWR/SC/D. It currently regulates the Kudankulam VVER-1000 units 1 to 6 and will apply, with technology-specific addenda, to integral pressurised water SMRs such as the Holtec SMR-300, the Westinghouse AP300, and any future NuScale VOYGR or BSR-200 designs proposed for Indian sites. AERB has publicly stated that the existing framework is broadly applicable to SMRs through a graded approach, with specific provisions being developed for modular factory fabrication, passive safety system reliability and multi-module siting.

faqs.question: What is the Safety Analysis Report and how does it evolve through the licensing process?
faqs.answer: The Safety Analysis Report is the central technical submission. It evolves through four versions: a Site Safety Report at the siting stage; a Preliminary Safety Analysis Report at construction consent; a Pre-Operational Safety Analysis Report before fuel loading; and a Final Safety Analysis Report as the as-built document that supports the operating licence. The FSAR must demonstrate plant response to all postulated initiating events, design basis accidents and design extension conditions, and must confirm that radiation doses to workers and the public remain within AERB limits. The FSAR is also updated at every ten-year Periodic Safety Review.

faqs.question: How is the first approach to criticality regulated?
faqs.answer: First approach to criticality is the most safety-significant commissioning milestone. It falls within Phase C of the commissioning programme and requires a specific written authorisation from AERB. The licensee submits the fuel loading programme, the approach to criticality procedure, the predicted critical configuration, the low-power physics test plan, and the Pre-Operational Safety Analysis Report. AERB conducts a multi-tier review and issues the clearance only after all stipulated safety requirements are closed. The AERB clearance for First Approach to Criticality at Kakrapar Unit 4 was issued by order dated 11 December 2023, with first criticality attained on 17 December 2023.

faqs.question: Are SMRs licensed under a separate code or under the existing AERB framework?
faqs.answer: Small modular reactors are licensed under the existing AERB framework, applied on a graded basis. AERB has stated that its existing regulatory requirements can generally be applied to advanced reactors including SMRs, with technology-specific provisions being developed for modular factory fabrication, transport of complete modules, multi-module siting, passive safety system reliability and below-grade integral pressurised water configurations. The siting code AERB/NF/SC/S, the design codes AERB/NPP-PHWR/SC/D and AERB/NPP-LWR/SC/D, and the operation code AERB/NPP/SC/O all apply to SMRs.

faqs.question: Can a foreign reactor vendor obtain an AERB licence directly?
faqs.answer: Under the SHANTI Act 2025, licences and authorisations to construct, own, operate and decommission a nuclear facility may be granted to private companies, joint ventures, government enterprises and other authorised entities. A foreign vendor will typically participate through an Indian incorporated joint venture or special purpose vehicle that holds the AERB consents. The vendor remains directly subject to AERB inspection at fabrication facilities outside India where safety-significant components are manufactured. Sensitive fuel cycle activities remain reserved for government entities.

faqs.question: How does AERB inspect vendor premises outside India?
faqs.answer: Construction quality assurance under AERB/NPP/SG/QA-1 requires AERB witness of major fabrication holds. For SMRs and advanced reactors where significant fabrication occurs at vendor factories abroad, the applicant must agree an inspection access protocol with AERB before construction consent is granted. This protocol typically covers reactor pressure vessel forging, integral steam generator fabrication, reactor module assembly, hydrostatic and helium leak testing, and module transport readiness. The cost of vendor premises inspections is borne by the applicant.

faqs.question: What is a Periodic Safety Review and how often is it required?
faqs.answer: Periodic Safety Review is a comprehensive re-evaluation of an operating nuclear power plant against current safety standards. AERB requires a brief safety review every five years for operating licence renewal and a comprehensive PSR every ten years. The PSR follows the fourteen safety factors framework derived from IAEA SSG-25, covering plant design, actual condition of structures, systems and components, equipment qualification, ageing management, deterministic and probabilistic safety analyses, hazard re-evaluation, safety performance, operating experience, organisation, procedures, human factors, emergency planning and environmental impact. PSR is also the vehicle for approving long-term operation beyond the original design life.

faqs.question: How long does an AERB licence remain valid?
faqs.answer: An operating licence for a nuclear power plant is issued for an initial period of five years. Renewal is conditional on satisfactory regulatory performance over the licence period and on the outcome of the Periodic Safety Review. Renewal is not automatic. AERB may attach additional conditions at renewal, may require corrective actions to be completed before renewal, and may decline to renew where safety performance is inadequate. Siting consent is open ended but lapses if construction is not commenced within a reasonable period. Construction sub-clearances are tied to specific milestones.

faqs.question: What does AERB require for radioactive waste management?
faqs.answer: Radioactive waste management is governed by AERB Safety Code AERB/NRF/SC/RW on Management of Radioactive Waste, supported by safety guides AERB/NRF/SG/RW-2 on predisposal management, AERB/NRF/SG/RW-4 on near surface disposal, AERB/RF/SG/RW-6 on spent radioactive sources and AERB/NPP&RR/SG/RW-8 on decommissioning. Solid waste is emplaced in approved near surface disposal facilities or, for high level waste, ultimately in deep geological repositories. The waste management plan is reviewed at every consent stage and is updated at every Periodic Safety Review.

faqs.question: How is decommissioning regulated under the SHANTI Act 2025?
faqs.answer: Decommissioning is the final regulatory consent. Technical requirements are set out in AERB Safety Manual AERB/SM/DECOM-1, in safety guide AERB/NPP&RR/SG/RW-8, and in the radioactive waste code AERB/NRF/SC/RW. The licensee submits a Decommissioning Plan addressing strategy, spent fuel removal, decontamination, waste characterisation, dose budgets and end-state criteria. The SHANTI Act 2025 also requires every operator to maintain a Decommissioning Financial Provision accumulated from the operating phase. For private and foreign operators this provision is a licence condition, and failure to maintain it is a ground for suspension of the operating consent.

faqs.question: How does the AERB framework compare to the US NRC Part 53 framework?
faqs.answer: Both frameworks aim to be technology-inclusive and graded. The US NRC finalised Part 53 in March 2026 as a risk-informed, performance-based alternative to Part 50 and Part 52 for advanced reactors and SMRs. AERB does not currently operate a separate technology-inclusive part. It applies the existing AERB/SC/G umbrella code and the LWR or PHWR design codes on a graded basis. The Indian PSAR conventions for postulated initiating events, single failure criterion and emergency core cooling system performance criteria are in places more prescriptive than the equivalent NRC requirements. Foreign vendors should not assume that an NRC certified design satisfies AERB without code-by-code mapping.

faqs.question: What should an Indian SMR developer or foreign vendor do first?
faqs.answer: Engage AERB during the conceptual siting study rather than at submission of the Site Safety Report. Map the design against AERB/NF/SC/S, AERB/NPP-LWR/SC/D or AERB/NPP-PHWR/SC/D, AERB/NPP/SC/O, AERB/NRF/SC/RW and the relevant safety guides. Build the PSAR around AERB code clauses from the outset rather than adapting an NRC or CNSC SAR. Secure an inspection access protocol for any non-Indian fabrication site before construction consent. Budget for AERB witness of vendor factory holds. Align the decommissioning financial provision with licence conditions from the date the operating licence is granted.
